questions.question: How does the course describe a 'complaint', as distinct from a whistleblowing concern?
options: A concern usually raised by a person who uses the service, or their family, about the quality of care they have personally received
A disclosure that can only lawfully be made to the CQC
A personal workplace issue affecting your own pay, rota or treatment
A concern raised in the public interest about wrongdoing that could harm others

questions.question: Under the Freedom to Speak Up principle of 'Openness', how should concerns be viewed?
options: As a sign of disloyalty to the team
As a gift that helps care improve
As a nuisance to be managed quietly
As a matter only for the police

questions.question: Winterbourne View, cited as a serious care failing where warning signs went unheard, was a hospital for people with:
options: dementia
learning disabilities
acute infections
physical injuries

questions.question: Whistleblowing protections under PIDA are described as 'day one' rights because:
options: the protection lasts only for your first day of employment
you must report any wrongdoing within one day of seeing it
they only apply after one year of service
you are protected from your very first day at work, with no minimum length of service required

questions.question: Which of the following is one of the qualifying categories of wrongdoing a protected disclosure can be about?
options: Dissatisfaction with your own pay
A colleague being promoted ahead of you
Damage to the environment
A disagreement over your rota

questions.question: According to the tiers of disclosure, making a wider disclosure — for example to the media — is:
options: the recommended first step
treated the same as telling your line manager
never allowed under any circumstances
allowed only in limited and stricter circumstances

questions.question: Since the law was updated in 2013, what is the position on a whistleblower acting in 'good faith'?
options: You no longer have to prove good faith, though bad faith can reduce any award you might receive
Your motives can never be questioned by a tribunal
You must prove good faith or lose all protection
You are protected only if a manager confirms your good faith in writing

questions.question: If a worker is dismissed because they made a protected disclosure, the law treats that dismissal as:
options: a fair dismissal
a personal grievance only
automatically unfair
a matter solely for the police

questions.question: In the course, 'Protect' is best described as:
options: a whistleblowing charity offering free, confidential advice
a government inspection body
a trade union for care workers
the employment tribunal service

questions.question: The barrier to speaking up summed up as 'that is just how things are done here' is described as:
options: Loyalty
Doubt
Fear
Habit

questions.question: If you are unsure whether an issue is a complaint, a grievance or a whistleblowing concern, the course advises you to:
options: stay silent until you are completely certain which it is
act only if it is definitely whistleblowing
raise the concern and let it be directed to the right process
wait for a colleague to report it first

questions.question: A disadvantage of raising a concern anonymously (giving no name at all) is that:
options: it is unlawful in adult social care
it removes all of your legal protection
it is harder to investigate the concern, or to give you feedback and support
it automatically triggers a police investigation

questions.question: Alongside the CQC, which of the following is named as an appropriate professional regulator that can receive a concern?
options: A national newspaper, as the first step
The Nursing and Midwifery Council
The person suspected of the wrongdoing
Your next-door neighbour

questions.question: In the realistic scenario, you notice a colleague regularly signs the medication chart before residents have taken their tablets. The course shows the best action is to:
options: write down the dates and exactly what you saw, then raise it with your manager the same day
wait several weeks until you are absolutely certain before acting
confront her privately and then let the matter drop
say nothing, because you like her and dread being seen as a troublemaker

questions.question: Since 2013, a whistleblower must still prove they acted in 'good faith' in order to be protected at all.
options: True
False

questions.question: A gagging or confidentiality clause in a contract or settlement can be lawfully used to stop you making a protected disclosure.
options: True
False

questions.question: Raising a genuine concern with the CQC, where it can act on it, can still count as a protected disclosure.
options: True
False

questions.question: If a worker is dismissed because of a protected disclosure, an employment tribunal can order compensation or reinstatement.
options: True
False

questions.question: Which of the following are described as barriers that can hold care workers back from speaking up? (Select all that apply)
options: A legal requirement to stay silent about wrongdoing
A financial reward for keeping quiet
Loyalty — not wanting to get a colleague or employer into trouble
Fear of losing your job or being blamed
Doubt that you are right or that anyone will believe you

questions.question: Which of the following are named in the course as sources of support or advice for someone with a concern? (Select all that apply)
options: The person you are raising the concern about
Protect, a whistleblowing charity
An anonymous social media group
Your manager
Your union, if you are a member

questions.question: Which of the following are steps you can expect after you raise a concern? (Select all that apply)
options: Feedback on the outcome, as far as is possible
Acknowledgement that your concern has been heard
Assessment and, where needed, investigation
Immediate dismissal of the person who raised it
A guaranteed cash payment

questions.question: Which of the following are examples of concerns that whistleblowing would cover in a care setting? (Select all that apply)
options: A personal dispute about your own shift rota
Dangerous staffing levels or missed medication
A colleague falsifying records or covering up a mistake
Your dislike of a manager's communication style
Abuse or neglect of a person who uses the service

questions.question: The deliberate ___ (cover-up) of wrongdoing is itself one of the qualifying categories under PIDA.
Protect is a whistleblowing ___ that offers free, confidential advice to people thinking about speaking up.
The Freedom to Speak Up approach describes concerns as a ___ that helps care improve.
